msContents.summary: Funerary text written for Nesypaheran, a priest ("scribe and oblationer of the House of Amun... god's father Ma-at") of the god Amun, preserving the Litany of Re.

supportDesc.extent:
A complete papyrus roll written along the fibres with the back being blank. The papyrus is mounted on paper and housed in four glass cases.
240 2440

layoutDesc.layout: Written in 23 columns with an illustration following each unit of text.

handDesc.handNote: Likely written by one scribe, all in black ink in "Book of the Dead cursive".

decoDesc.decoNote: A series of illustrations are included in the text following each text unit.

history.provenance: Possibly from Thebes. Purchased by Colonel Valentine Patrick Terrell Vivian in Egypt around 1882 and bequeathed to his brother-in-law the Reverend Vivian Eccles Skrine.
history.acquisition: Given to the Bodleian by the Reverend Vivian Eccles Skrine (1850-1925), 30 March 1913.

recordHist.source: Summary description abbreviated from the following sources: Blackman, Aylward M., “The Funerary Papyrus of Nesypaheran (Pap. Skrine, No. 2),” Journal of Egyptian Archaeology 5.1 (1918), 24–35.

availability.p: This item may only be consulted by prior arrangement with a curator. To make an application, please click the request button above. When your request is received, you will be asked to contact the relevant person.

listBibl.head: Printed descriptions:
listBibl.bibl: Blackman, Aylward M., “The Funerary Papyrus of Nespeḥer'an (Pap. Skrine, No. 2),” Journal of Egyptian Archaeology 5.1 (1918), 24–35.
Niwiński, Andrzej, Studies on the Illustrated Theban Funerary Papyri of the 11th and 10th Centuries B.C. (Freiburg (Schweiz) and Göttingen: Universitätsverlag, 1989), p. 349.online copy.
Stevens, Marissa, Shaping Identities in the Context of Crisis: The Social Self Reflected in 21st Dynasty Funerary Papyri (PhD diss., University of California, Los Angeles, [year]), .

msItem.note: The beginning of the papyrus showing the soul of the deceased addressing four deities.

supportDesc.extent: The first part of the funerary papyrus. ?? ??

decoDesc.decoNote: The first frame starts with the representation of the soul ("ba") of the deceased Nespeheran, its head surmounted with a dab of scented ointment and a lotus bud, it is positioned in front a lighted lamp. This is followed by the first deity addressed, represented as a long snake coiling in the frame called Seneh-her; the second image shows a disk containing a ram's head with a uraeus snake between its horns called "Radiance"; the third one shows a bird in a sun-disk with a cat below it, called the "Soul of Ra"; the fourth represents a lion- or panther-headed god, called Netet-efem-neket.

msItem.note: The second part of the papyrus showing six deities addressed with praises.

supportDesc.extent: The second part of the funerary papyrus. ?? ??

decoDesc.decoNote: The second frame starts with an image showing a mummiform divinity with a head of a griffin, called "Lord of Strength"; this is followed by a crocodile-headed divinity with averted face, called "Turned Face"; this is followed by a mummiform figure with a a burning lamp as its head called the "Great Heat", the next image shows a goat seated on its haunches in a mock-human position called the "Ram"; the penultimate image shows a mummiform divinity with the head of bubale called "He Whose Body is Hidden"; the last image is a mummiform deity with a bearded ram's head called "The Great Flood".

msItem.note: The third part of the papyrus showing seven deities addressed with praises.

supportDesc.extent: The second part of the funerary papyrus. ?? ??

decoDesc.decoNote: The third frame starts with an image of a deity in the form of a captive, kneeling on one knee with his arms bound behind his back called the "Vanquished One"; the next image is a godess with two heads, the one of a lioness, the other of a crocodile called "Saryt"; the next one shows a ram-headed beetle, surmounted by the solar disk between two uraeus snakes called the "Venerated Power"; the next one is an ox-headed mummiform deity named the "Bull of the West"; the following image shows the sacred eye in a circle called the "Divine Eye"; the penultimate one shows a mummiform deity called the "Hidden One"; the last image shows a mummiform divinity with a donkey's head, who grasps a gecko-lizard with both hands called "Mysterious of Modes of Being".

msItem.note: The fourth part of the papyrus showing six deities addressed with praises.

supportDesc.extent: The second part of the funerary papyrus. ?? ??

decoDesc.decoNote: The fourth frame starts with the image of a lion-headed mummiform divinity with a live snake in his mouth called the "Hidden One"; the next one is another mummiform divinity, whose had is a pot issuing water called "Divine Water"; this is followed by another mummified figure with the head of a hare called "He Who Lets His Voice Resound"; this is followed by another mummiform figure with a basket of onions and bread as its head, called "Lord of Bread"; the penultimate image is a snake-headed mummiform god called the "Righteous One" and the last one shows a cat seated on its haunches above the sacred eye, accompanied by a scarab called "Cat of Lapis Lazuli".
